text: The video shows a large diaphragmatic hernia defect with the majority of the stomach visibly herniated into the chest cavity.
The video shows a large diaphragmatic hernia defect with the majority of the stomach visibly herniated into the chest cavity.
The surgeon makes an incision in the peritoneum overlying the hiatal defect at the one o'clock position, initiating the robotic paraesophageal hernia repair.
The surgeon makes an incision in the peritoneum overlying the hiatal defect at the one o'clock position, initiating the robotic paraesophageal hernia repair.
The surgeon extends the incision over to the left side of the screen, revealing a bloodless plane to sweep the hernia sac down and away from the endothoracic fascia.
The surgeon extends the incision over to the left side of the screen, identifying a bloodless plane to carefully sweep the hernia sac down and away from the endothoracic fascia.
The beating heart is visible above, covered by the pericardium, as the surgeon continues the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon exposes the beating heart, which is visible above and covered by the pericardium.
The surgeon incises and opens the gastrohepatic ligament widely, continuing the external dissection around the hiatal area.
The surgeon incises and opens the gastrohepatic ligament widely, continuing the external dissection around the hiatal area.
The surgeon carefully preserves the left gastric artery while an accessory left hepatic artery is visible, crossing over the green-tinted tissue.
The surgeon carefully preserves the left gastric artery while an accessory left hepatic artery is visible, traversing towards the green left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon grasps the hernia sac and retracts it downward out of the chest, while dividing adhesions between the sac and surrounding structures, including the left pleura, which houses the lung.

The surgeon grasps the hernia sac and retracts it downward out of the chest, while dividing adhesions between the hernia sac and surrounding structures, including the left pleura, which houses the lung.

After completing the initial dissection of the anterior half of the hernia, the surgeon moves down the greater curvature of the stomach. Using a vessel sealer, they access the lesser sac, the space located behind the stomach.

The surgeon completes the initial dissection of the anterior half of the hernia and proceeds about a third to halfway down the greater curvature of the stomach. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon enters the lesser sac, located behind the stomach.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect upward towards the esophagus, carefully taking the short gastric vessels and posterior gastric vessels visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect upward towards the esophagus, taking the short gastric vessels and posterior gastric vessels visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon fully mobilizes the upper part of the stomach, including the fundus, preparing it for subsequent fundoplication.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the upper part of the stomach, including the fundus, preparing it for future fundoplication. Blood vessels tethering the stomach are divided to facilitate this mobilization.
The surgeon continues to divide the blood vessels tethering the stomach, using a vessel sealer to carefully separate these structures from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses an advanced energy source to carefully divide vessels, minimizing bleeding while separating the hernia sac from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses an advanced energy source to carefully divide blood vessels, minimizing bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon navigates around the lower esophagus and upper stomach, ensuring complete circumferential access to these structures as part of the early steps in the operation.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the lower esophagus and upper stomach, preparing to pass a Penrose drain to encircle and control the hiatal contents.
The surgeon passes a Penrose drain around the hiatal contents to encircle and control them, following the mobilization of the upper stomach.
The surgeon uses a Penrose drain to encircle the hiatal contents, providing a handle for effective retraction, which facilitates the remainder of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon uses the mobilized upper stomach, including the fundus, as a handle for effective retraction, facilitating the continuation of the operation.
The surgeon continues to dissect the hernia sac posteriorly, carefully separating it from the cruse using precise techniques.
The surgeon continues to dissect the hernia sac posteriorly, carefully separating it from the crura to reduce the risk of hernia recurrence.
The surgeon continues dissection high up in the mediastinum, carefully separating tissue layers around the esophagus and upper stomach.
The surgeon continues dissection high up in the mediastinum, performing extensive mobilization to free the esophagus from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs extensive mediastinal mobilization, carefully freeing the esophagus from surrounding tissue to facilitate further surgical steps.
The surgeon operates in a small working space using articulated instruments, which facilitate precise movements and manipulation of tissues.
The surgeon disassembles the penders to assess the size of the hernia sac, visually examining its dimensions within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the hernia sac from the esophagus and gastroesophageal junction, carefully removing it from the body.
The surgeon dissects the hernia sac from the esophagus and gastroesophageal junction, carefully removing it from the body.
The surgeon carefully dissects the remaining portion of the hernia sac, separating it from surrounding tissues using articulated instruments within a confined working space.

The surgeon carefully dissects the final portion of the hernia sac, which is adherent to the right cruce, using precise movements to avoid entering the right pleural space or the cavity containing the right lung.
The surgeon carefully dissects the portion of the hernia sac that is adherent to the right crus, using articulated instruments to navigate the confined surgical space.
The surgeon carefully teases apart the hernia sac, ensuring not to enter the right pleural space or the cavity containing the right lung.
The surgeon uses robotic assistance to zoom into the space between anatomical structures, ensuring the area is not violated during the dissection.
The surgeon uses robotic assistance to zoom into the space between the hernia sac and surrounding structures, ensuring the space is not violated during dissection.
The surgeon observes the crura coming together well after completing the external hiatal dissection and mediastinal mobilization, deciding that mesh reinforcement is not necessary.

The surgeon completes the external hiatal dissection and mediastinal mobilization, observing that the crura come together well, eliminating the need for mesh. Xeroethabones are introduced into the abdomen for the hiatal hernia repair.
The surgeon introduces xeroethabones into the abdomen as part of the hiatal hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon measures the intraabdominal length of the esophagus, ensuring it is more than three centimeters so that the gastroesophageal junction is positioned well below the diaphragm.

The surgeon measures the intraabdominal length of the esophagus, ensuring it is more than three centimeters so that the gastroesophageal junction is positioned below the diaphragm. The hiatal hernia repair is initiated using several interrupted sutures of zero ethebond.
The surgeon begins the hiatal hernia repair by placing several interrupted sutures using zero ethebond to approximate the crura.
The surgeon observes the muscular pillars of the diaphragm coming together without significant tension, indicating successful approximation during the hiatal hernia repair.
The surgeon observes the muscular pillars of the diaphragm coming together without excessive tension, indicating that mesh reinforcement is not necessary for this repair.
The surgeon continues the hiatal hernia repair using robotic assistance, demonstrating fluid and precise movements as the robotic arms facilitate the surgical maneuvers.
The surgeon continues the hiatal hernia repair using robotic assistance, demonstrating fluid and precise movements during the procedure.
During the hiatal hernia repair, the surgeon observes increased tension and uses a vessel sealer to make a semi-relaxing incision along the left side to alleviate it.

During the hiatal hernia repair, the surgeon observes increased tension and makes a semi-relaxing incision along the left side using a vessel sealer. This incision facilitates the muscle to come together at the midline more easily.
The surgeon completes the maneuver, facilitating the approximation of the muscle fibers at the midline, visibly aiding in the closure of the hiatal hernia defect.
The surgeon assesses the esophagus's position within the hiatal defect, visually evaluating the extent to which the esophagus fills the defect during the hiatal hernia repair.

The surgeon assesses the esophagus filling the hiatal defect, deciding to place two sutures anteriorly to close the hiatus to a normal size. This technique aims to avoid excessive angulation of the esophagus, which could lead to swallowing difficulties.
The surgeon places two sutures anteriorly in front of the esophagus to close the hiatal defect, ensuring the hiatus returns to a normal size without excessive angulation that could impede swallowing.
The surgeon completes the hiatal hernia repair and begins the partial posterior fundoplication, positioning the stomach fundus behind the esophagus.

The surgeon completes the hiatal hernia repair and begins the partial posterior fundoplication by passing the fundus behind the esophagus from left to right. The surgeon then performs a shoeshine maneuver to ensure the wrap is symmetric and without twisting at the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon passes the fundus of the stomach behind the esophagus from left to right, then grasps an equidistant part on the patient's left side to perform a shoeshine maneuver.
The surgeon performs a gastropexy by suturing the stomach to the abdominal wall, securing its position.
The surgeon performs a gastropexy by suturing the posterior aspect of the fundoplication wrap to the corral closure, anchoring the wrap within the abdomen to prevent a recurrent hiatal hernia.
The surgeon secures the backside of the wrap to the corral closure, ensuring proper alignment and stability of the surgical repair.
The surgeon anchors the fundoplication within the abdomen, securing it to prevent a recurrent hiatal hernia.
The surgeon completes the final adjustments on the fundoplication, which visually resembles a hot dog-like shape, indicating the intended configuration of the wrap.
The surgeon completes the fundoplication, which visually resembles a hot dog-like shape externally. Intraoperative endoscopy confirms the internal appearance of an omega loop.
The surgeon uses intraoperative endoscopy to check the internal appearance of the fundoplication, which resembles an omega loop.
The surgeon inspects the surgical site for hemostasis, ensuring there is no active bleeding and that the anatomical structures appear as expected.

The surgeon checks for hemostasis and visually inspects the surgical site to ensure proper appearance. An endoscope is used to illuminate and verify the position of the gastroesophageal junction and the level of the fundoplication.
The endoscopic light illuminates the surgical field, allowing the surgeon to visually check the location of the gastroesophageal junction and assess the level of the fundoplication.
